coredata.dc:title: Comparison of hydrogen absorption properties of Ti mechanically milled with hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN) and with graphite
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2004 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Kondo, Toshihiko
Shindo, Kazuhiko
Sakurai, Yoji
coredata.dc:description: We compared the structural and hydrogen absorption properties of Ti mechanically milled with hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN) with those obtained when Ti was milled with graphite. With increasing milling time, the N and C atoms originating from h-BN and graphite dissolved into the interstitial sites of Ti, leading to the formation of the interstitial solid solutions, TiN and TiC, respectively. An investigation with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) suggested the existence of an interaction between Ti and N or C atoms due to their interstitial dissolution on the Ti surface. Milling with h-BN improved the kinetics of the hydrogen absorption of Ti as well as milling with graphite although the former required a longer milling time for full activation. The improvement in the kinetics can probably be attributed to the interstitial dissolution of N or C atoms on the Ti surface, which may provide the pathways for hydrogen penetration into Ti bulk. We revealed that the existence of the interstitial atoms on the Ti surface is an important factor in relation to the improvement in the hydrogen absorption kinetics of Ti.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrogen storage material
Titanium
Hexagonal boron nitride
Graphite
Mechanical milling

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Properties of Ti surfaces in composites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of (a) sample A and (b) sample B milled for various times.
Ti 2p XPS spectra for (a) Ti milled without additives for 5h, (b) sample A milled for 5h, and (c) sample B milled for 5h. The dashed line indicates the binding energy for normal metallic Ti.
Dependence of Ti 2p XPS spectra on milling time. Sample A milled for (a) 20h and (b) 5h. Sample B milled for (c) 20h and (d) 5h.
Dependence of hydrogen absorption capacity of Ti on milling time at 25°C under a hydrogen pressure of 0.8MPa (○) in sample A and (•) in sample B.
XRD patterns after hydrogen absorption. (a) Sample A milled for 1h and (b) sample B milled for 1h.

Hydrogen absorption curves of various Ti at 25°C under a hydrogen pressure of 0.8MPa. (+) Ti milled without additives for 5h, sample A milled for (○) 1h, (▵) 2h, (♢) 5h, and (▿) 20h; sample B milled for (•) 1h, (▴) 2h, () 5h, and (▾) 20h.

converted-article.ce:title: Comparison of hydrogen absorption properties of Ti mechanically milled with hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN) and with graphite

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: NTT Microsystem Integration Laboratories, Nippon Telegraph and Telephone Corporation, 3-1 Morinosato-Wakamiya, Atsugi-shi, Kanagawa 243-0198, Japan

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Present address: NTT R&D Strategy Department, NTT Corporation, 3-1, Otemachi 2-Chome Chiyoda-ku, Tokyo 100-8116, Japan.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We compared the structural and hydrogen absorption properties of Ti mechanically milled with hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN) with those obtained when Ti was milled with graphite. With increasing milling time, the N and C atoms originating from h-BN and graphite dissolved into the interstitial sites of Ti, leading to the formation of the interstitial solid solutions, TiN and TiC, respectively. An investigation with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) suggested the existence of an interaction between Ti and N or C atoms due to their interstitial dissolution on the Ti surface. Milling with h-BN improved the kinetics of the hydrogen absorption of Ti as well as milling with graphite although the former required a longer milling time for full activation. The improvement in the kinetics can probably be attributed to the interstitial dissolution of N or C atoms on the Ti surface, which may provide the pathways for hydrogen penetration into Ti bulk. We revealed that the existence of the interstitial atoms on the Ti surface is an important factor in relation to the improvement in the hydrogen absorption kinetics of Ti.

ce:section.ce:para: Mg and Ti are well known as lightweight hydrogen storage materials and hydrogen can be stored more safely and compactly in them than in its compressed or liquid forms. These materials are usually covered with thin layers of oxides or hydroxides and require activation treatment under high temperature (>300°C) and hydrogen pressure (>3MPa) before being used for storage and transport applications [1–4].

Various Mg and Ti activation methods have been proposed based on surface modification. For instance, Mg modified by polycyclic aromatic compounds has good hydrogen absorption kinetics without being heated in a hydrogen atmosphere [5]. Mg or Ti mechanically milled with graphite, which has a polycyclic aromatic framework, also exhibit improved hydrogen absorption kinetics [6,7]. These modifications are effective for some alloys containing Mg or Ti [7–9]. It has been concluded that one of the reasons for the improvements in Mg systems is the interaction between Mg and aromatic compounds with charge transfer, which promotes the activation of hydrogen molecules [6,8,9]. However, there has been no detailed examination of Mg and Ti modified by compounds other than carbon-based ones.

In this study, we focused on h-BN as an additive for the modification of hydrogen storage materials. h-BN and graphite have similar layered structures and mechanical properties, but different electronic properties; h-BN is an insulator and graphite is a semimetal as a result of the differences in their electronic band structures [10,11]. We can use h-BN as a probe for investigating the influence of electronic properties on modification. In addition, the hydrogen absorption properties in the Ti–h-BN system were unknown although the solid-phase reaction of Ti with h-BN caused by mechanical milling has been reported [12]. We evaluated the structural and hydrogen absorption properties of Ti mechanically milled with h-BN and compared them with those of Ti milled with graphite.

Ti powder (Furuuchi Chemical, <150μm, 99.9%) and h-BN (Denka, Denka Boron Nitride, 7μm, 99%) or graphite powder (Kansai Coke and Chemical, NG-7, 7μm, 99.99%) were mixed with a composition of Ti–50wt.% h-BN or graphite (weight ratio = 1:1). The mixture was placed in a tempered Cr-steel bowl (45ml) with a sealing o-ring together with 7mm diameter Cr–steel balls and then sealed in an argon atmosphere. Milling was carried out using a planetary ball mill apparatus (Fritsch, Pulverisette 7) at a rotation speed of 400rpm for 1–80h. The ball to powder weight ratio was 30:1. All the prepared samples were handled in a glove box filled with purified argon to avoid the influence of oxidation and moisture.

The structures of the prepared samples were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku, MultiFlex with Cu Kα radiation) measurements. The surface state of the prepared samples was evaluated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI, XPS5700 with Al Kα radiation). Some charged samples were neutralized with an electron gun. The C 1p peak of graphite or hydrocarbon (existing as background) at 284.6eV was used to calibrate the peak positions. The peaks were identified on the basis of reported values [13–17]. The hydrogen absorption properties of the prepared samples were determined by the conventional volumetric method under a hydrogen pressure of 0.8MPa using an automatic Sievert apparatus (Lesca). The measurements were carried out at 25°C without any activation treatment at high temperature and hydrogen pressure. The purity of the hydrogen we used was 99.9999%. The sample weight for the measurement was 0.6g.

The XRD patterns of Ti–h-BN composite (sample A) and Ti–graphite composite (sample B) mechanically milled for various time periods are shown in Fig. 1 . The peaks attributed to h-BN and graphite weakened with increasing milling time and disappeared after milling for more than 5h. This indicates the disappearance of the long-range ordering of h-BN and graphite. There were no significant changes in the Ti peaks during the initial milling stage (up to 5 and 2h in samples A and B, respectively). After that, the Ti peaks weakened and the peaks attributed to TiN and TiC grew with increasing milling time in samples A and B, respectively. In both cases, only the peaks of the interstitial solid solutions were observed after milling for 80h. The formation of TiN and TiC is most likely due to the dissolution of N and C atoms originating from h-BN and graphite, respectively, into the interstitial sites of Ti. Another interstitial solid solution, TiB2, was not observed in sample A at any milling time in our study, although the formation of TiN/TiB2 by the mechanical milling of Ti with h-BN has been reported [12]. This difference is probably due to the milling conditions, especially the molar ratio of h-BN to Ti. When there is excess h-BN, the interstitial dissolution of N atoms is superior to that of B atoms because the atomic radius of N is almost the same as that of C and about 6% smaller than that of B. The residual B atoms may exist as amorphous B in the composites since no phases related to B atoms were detected by XRD. In sample A, a longer milling time was needed before the peaks of the corresponding interstitial solid solution appeared, indicating that Ti reacts with graphite more rapidly than with h-BN. We discuss the reason for this difference in the reactivity in the next section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Properties of Ti surfaces in composites
ce:section.ce:para: The surface state is an important factor that affects the kinetics of hydrogen absorption in hydrogen storage materials. We used XPS to evaluate the Ti surface in composites. Fig. 2 shows Ti 2p XPS spectra for host Ti, Ti milled without additives for 5h, and samples A and B milled for 5h. In host Ti, we observed the Ti 2p3/2 peak at 458.5eV attributed to TiO2 [13], indicating that host Ti is covered with a layer of TiO2 and that there is no metallic Ti on the surface. The new peak at 453.2eV after milling without additives corresponds to the appearance of metallic Ti on the surface [14]. This indicates that the TiO2 layer is destroyed by pulverization. Moreover, in samples A and B, similar peaks appeared at about 454.1eV, but the binding energy was about 0.9eV higher than that of normal metallic Ti, suggesting that milling with h-BN or graphite decreases the electron density around Ti. The decrease is probably due to the interaction between Ti and N or C atoms, in which N or C atoms begin to dissolve interstitially on the Ti surface. However, TiN and TiC are not formed on the Ti surface because the binding energies for Ti in both samples A and B were different from those for TiN and TiC, which are 455.2 and 454.9eV, respectively [15,16]. We believe that non-stoichiometric interstitial solid solutions are formed on the Ti surface. In fact, it has been reported that the Ti 2p3/2 peaks for TiN x (x = 0.12–0.96) are located between those for metallic Ti and TiN [17]. The peak of the interacted Ti in sample B was more prominent than that in sample A. The peak attributed to TiO2 was preserved in sample A, while its intensity was very weak in sample B. Even when the milling time was extended to 20h, we observed no significant changes in the shape of the spectra although the normalized peak intensities of the interacted Ti became strong, as shown in Fig. 3 . The changes in the peak intensities correspond to the increase in the interstitial N or C atoms on the surfaces of the composites. These results suggest that milling with graphite removes the TiO2 layer from the Ti surface more effectively, resulting in greater interstitial dissolution on the Ti surface than found with h-BN. This seems to explain why Ti reacts with graphite more rapidly than with h-BN. Above all, the Ti surfaces in samples A and B are essentially different. This is probably due to the difference between the electronic properties of h-BN and graphite. Unlike h-BN, graphite has π electrons with high mobility and can act as a π ligand [18]. Therefore, there is likely to be an interaction with the charge transfer between Ti and graphite, as reported for Mg–graphite and MgNi–graphite composites [6,9]. This interaction may play an important role in the removal of the TiO2 layer and the prevention of Ti reoxidation by residual oxygen.

We evaluated the capacity and kinetics of hydrogen absorption at 25°C for the samples after milling for various time periods to investigate the influence of milling with h-BN or graphite on the hydrogen absorption properties of Ti. Here, the amount of absorbed hydrogen was converted to a Ti weight basis by excluding the h-BN or graphite weights of all the composites.

Fig. 4 shows the dependence of the hydrogen absorption capacities of Ti on milling time in samples A and B. The hydrogen absorption capacities were approximately 4.2wt.% in both samples A and B milled for 1h. This value is equal to the theoretical hydrogen absorption of Ti. Moreover, peaks attributed to TiH2 and h-BN or graphite were observed in the XRD patterns of the samples after hydrogen absorption, as shown in Fig. 5 . These results support the view that Ti mainly absorbs hydrogen and that the hydrogen physisorption in h-BN or graphite is negligible under a hydrogen pressure of 0.8MPa. Although in both cases these hydrogen absorption capacities then decreased with increasing milling time, the decay of the capacity of sample A was slower than that of sample B. This tendency seems to correspond to the formation of interstitial solid solutions, as shown in Fig. 1. The interstitial N or C atoms probably occupy the sites for hydrogen absorption in Ti, resulting in the reduction of the hydrogen absorption capacities.

Fig. 6 shows the hydrogen absorption curves of Ti in samples A and B. The Ti in both samples A and B could absorb hydrogen rapidly even at 25°C, while Ti milled without h-BN and graphite could not in spite of the existence of metallic Ti on the surface. The hydrogen absorption kinetics for Ti in sample A was improved with increasing milling time to the same degree as that in sample B although a longer milling time was required for sample A. This result together with XPS analyses suggests that the improvement in the kinetics is independent of the existence of the metallic Ti, but is attributed to the quantity of interstitial N or C atoms on the Ti surface. It may be that the phase boundaries induced by the interstitial dissolution of N or C atoms on the Ti surface act as pathways for hydrogen penetration into Ti bulk. Prolonged milling increases the number of pathways on the Ti surface, leading to further improvement in the hydrogen absorption kinetics. A longer milling time is required for the improvement in sample A because milling with h-BN does not induce the interstitial dissolution on the Ti surface more efficiently than milling with graphite, as described above.

The hydrogen absorption kinetics of Ti was improved by milling with h-BN as well as by milling with graphite, although the difference between the electronic properties of h-BN and graphite affected the Ti surface in composites, resulting in different improvement behavior. We believe that the most important factor related to this improvement is the interstitial dissolution of N or C atoms, which originate from h-BN or graphite, on the Ti surface.

We investigated the structural and hydrogen absorption properties for Ti mechanically milled with h-BN for various milling times and compared the results with those obtained with graphite. The hydrogen absorption kinetics for both Ti milled with h-BN and Ti milled with graphite was improved with increasing milling time although a longer time was required with h-BN than with graphite. XPS analyses suggested that the improvement in the kinetics could be attributed to the interstitial dissolution of N or C atoms on the Ti surface, which may provide the pathways for hydrogen penetration into Ti bulk. We found that the improvement in the hydrogen absorption kinetics of Ti is not peculiar to milling with graphite. We consider that the improvement is essentially independent of the electronic properties of the additives, but depends on the interstitial atoms on the Ti surface.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: M.W. Mueller, Titanium hydrides, in: Metal Hydrides, Academic Press, New York, 1968 (Chapter 8).
R. Thomson, in: R.J. Brotherton, et al. (Eds.), Progress in Boron Chemistry, vol. 2, Pergamon Press, London, 1970, p. 173.
